title: Are Americans optimistic about their finances?
gist: Per the Bankrate Consumer Survey, US adults expecting their personal finances to worsen in the year ahead jumped from 12% (2018) to 32% (2025), while those expecting improvement fell from 44% to 34% — a deterioration in forward-looking confidence that, paired with still-positive current well-being (SHED), points to more cautious household spending and saving.

insights.claim: The share of US adults in the Bankrate Consumer Survey expecting their personal finances to worsen over the next year rose from 12% in 2018 to 32% in 2025.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A near-tripling of pessimists signals weakening consumer sentiment that historically precedes softer discretionary spending — watch consumer-facing and retail names.
insights.quote: the share of survey respondents expecting their finances to worsen grew from 12% to 32%

insights.claim: The share of US adults expecting their personal finances to improve in the coming year fell from 44% in 2018 to 34% in 2025, per the Bankrate Consumer Survey.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Optimists now barely outnumber pessimists (34% vs 32%), a narrowing margin that flags fragile consumer confidence as a macro risk.
insights.quote: the share of survey respondents expecting their finances to improve shrank from 44% to 34%

insights.claim: Expectations about personal finances are presented as the core driver of consumer confidence, which in turn shapes household spending, saving, and overall economic activity.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Frames year-ahead expectation surveys as a leading indicator for consumption — useful for anticipating GDP and demand turns before hard data prints.
insights.quote: Expectations about personal finance conditions are at the heart of consumer confidence

insights.claim: When households report broadly positive current well-being but tepid year-ahead optimism, the likely result is more cautious spending and saving.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A divergence between solid current conditions and weak forward optimism argues for a precautionary-savings tilt and softer consumption ahead — bearish for cyclical consumer demand.
insights.quote: When current well-being is broadly positive but year-ahead optimism is tepid, the result may be more cautious spending and saving by households.
insights.locator: Put together, the Bankrate and SHED surveys

insights.claim: The Bankrate Consumer Survey is conducted annually each November or December and weights responses to reflect the broad makeup of the US adult population, asking whether respondents expect their finances to be better, worse, or the same in the coming year.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Knowing the survey is annual and population-weighted helps calibrate how much weight to give the trend versus higher-frequency confidence gauges.

insights.claim: The deteriorating forward-looking Bankrate expectations complement the perceived economic well-being trend reported in the Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking (SHED), giving two corroborating views of present conditions and future confidence.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Cross-validation across two independent surveys strengthens the signal that household sentiment is softening, reducing the odds it is survey noise.
insights.quote: complements what we discussed in a recent FRED Blog post about the perceived economic well-being of households reported through the Survey of Household Economics and Decisionmaking (SHED)
insights.locator: Bankrate weights the collected survey responses
